text.content:
Phase 8 Pressure System

Phase 8 runs on two clocks rather than the single clock used in earlier phases. The party feels pressure from two different directions:

  • The Threshold Lockdown Clock — how heavily Korre's route-cleaners are watching this site. Advances when the party is loud, slow, or careless. Affects how many enemies are present and how alert they are.
  • The Structural Failure Clock — the threshold complex is 600+ years old and was sealed under hasty conditions in 542 CY. It is fragile. Advances when the party uses force or magical pressure. Affects whether the threshold survives long enough to be used for descent and return.

Why two clocks

Phase 8 is a heist and an archaeological dig. A party that focuses entirely on stealth (low Lockdown) but uses brute force on every obstacle (high Structural Failure) ends up in a different place from a party that loudly fights every cleaner they see (high Lockdown) but treats the stonework with care (low Structural Failure). Both paths are valid; both have consequences.

Reading the clocks at the table

The GM should track these openly on a piece of paper or a virtual tabletop counter visible to the party. The party should know how much pressure they are under. This is a tension-builder, not a hidden mechanism.

How clocks interact with the door scene

The Permission Door is the climactic moment for both clocks. The method the party chooses to open the door directly advances or holds these clocks (see the Permission Door journal). The clocks then carry forward into the ending package and into Phase 9.

Starting values

Both clocks start at 0 of 6. The party arrives at a quiet, neglected site. Pressure builds from their actions and from the world's response.

Threshold Lockdown Clock (0–6)

Tracks how thoroughly Korre's network is watching the Tarnsmere site. Higher values mean more cleaners, more alertness, more reinforcements on call from Greyhawk City.

What advances this clock

TriggerAdvance
Party arrives by Opening B (Triangulated Late)+1 (starting bump)
Party arrives by Opening C (Partial Triangulation)+1 (starting bump)
Party fires a weapon, casts a noisy spell, or shouts in the open+1 per occurrence
Tollhouse Yard encounter is fought rather than avoided+1
A cleaner agent is killed and the body is left where it can be found+1
A cleaner agent escapes alive and reports back+2
Party spends more than 4 hours of in-fiction time at the site+1 per additional 4-hour block
Forcing the Permission Door (Method E)+1
Using forged Korre Papers (Method D) without disabling the listening tube+1
Falling Stair Trap (Subsurface Location 7) triggers+1 (the crack echoes loudly)

What holds this clock

  • Quiet movement, stealth, scouting.
  • Knocking out a cleaner cleanly and binding them out of sight.
  • Approaching by the Cistern Drain bypass (cleaners do not watch it).
  • Avoiding the Tollhouse Yard entirely (using the dry well, Subsurface Location 5 alternate entry).

Effects by segment

Lockdown 0–1: Light Watch

  • One Silent Scout patrols the surface every 30 minutes, lazily.
  • The Tollhouse Yard has 2 hired toughs idling at the brazier.
  • No reinforcements are on call.

Lockdown 2: Active Watch

  • Two Silent Scouts on the surface, with overlapping patrol patterns.
  • The Tollhouse Yard has 3 hired toughs and 1 Route-Cleaner Agent.
  • Recent paperwork burning evident in the Broken Registry Chamber (half-burned packets, heat-reactive ink residue).
  • One messenger is dispatched to Greyhawk City to report. Reinforcements will arrive in 12 hours.

Lockdown 3: Lockdown

  • Three Silent Scouts coordinate; one is always within signal range of the Tollhouse.
  • The Tollhouse Yard has 4 hired toughs and 2 Route-Cleaner Agents under a Route-Cleaner Lead.
  • The pressure trap on the landing stair (Subsurface Location 7) is armed.
  • The Permission Door's listening tube is being actively monitored on a regular check.
  • Reinforcements arrive in 6 hours.

Lockdown 4: Hard Lockdown

  • The full Tollhouse complex is staffed. Yard and surface contain 5 hired toughs, 3 Route-Cleaner Agents, 1 Lead, and 1 Deep-Route Courier on standby.
  • The Cistern Drain is also being checked — one Silent Scout is watching the cistern outflow point.
  • Reinforcements arrive in 3 hours, in 2 waves of 4 cleaners each.

Lockdown 5: Manhunt

  • The site is functionally besieged. Any PC seen on the surface is engaged on sight.
  • Reinforcements arrive continuously, in waves of 4 cleaners every 2 hours.
  • The party's faces are being circulated in Greyhawk City. Future phases inherit a +2 to all encounter-difficulty rolls in the city for ten in-fiction days.

Lockdown 6: Burn the Site

  • Korre has decided the site is compromised beyond salvage. The Tollhouse is being prepared for full collapse — cleaners are setting structural fuses in the upper ruin to bring the building down on the entry.
  • The party has 30 in-fiction minutes to either descend through the Permission Door (sealing themselves below) or evacuate the site entirely.
  • If the party is still in the surface or subsurface complex when the fuses fire, the site collapses. The party must make a hard call: descend, run, or take the collapse and try to dig out.

What “reinforcements arrive” means in play

Reinforcements appear as additional Route-Cleaner Agents and Silent Scouts on the surface, with messengers running between Tarnsmere and Greyhawk City. The party can see them coming if they have lookouts posted (Perception DC 13 on the spur road).

Structural Failure Clock (0–6)

Tracks how badly the 600-year-old threshold complex is damaged by the party's activity. Higher values mean more partial collapses, more dust, more visible damage that route-cleaners will read on their next sweep, and more chance the descent is irreparable.

What advances this clock

TriggerAdvance
Forcing the Permission Door (Method E)+2
Cistern Drain bypass when Structural Failure is already 2+ (water surge)+1
Knock spell on the Permission Door+1
Pulverising a wall to break a counter chain+2
Bringing down a passage section to escape pursuit+1
Heavy combat in the Old Guardroom (Subsurface Location 10) with ranged force damage+1
Setting a fire inside the complex (route-cleaners doing it counts)+1
Falling Stair Trap (pressure trigger) firing+1 (small block damages ceiling)
Each in-fiction hour the Lockdown Clock is at 6 and the party is still inside+1 (the cleaners are dropping fuses)

What holds this clock

  • Treating the stonework with care.
  • Using paperwork methods to open the door (A, B, C, D, Elle).
  • Avoiding noisy spells inside the complex.
  • Not setting fires.

Effects by segment

Structural 0–1: Stable

The complex is its 600-year-old self. Some loose stones, some damp corners, nothing immediate. Movement is unimpeded.

Structural 2: Hairline Cracking

  • New hairline cracks appear in the upper passage walls and the Stair Gallery landings.
  • The Cistern Drain shaft begins to leak more water (water surge mechanism is now active).
  • Route-cleaners can read recent damage and infer activity. Lockdown Clock will advance +1 from any cleaner sweep that finds the cracks.

Structural 3: Loose Stones

  • Walking through certain corridors dislodges small stones from the ceiling.
  • Any creature traversing the Old Mining Spur Road approach in heavy gear makes a Dexterity save DC 12 once per session at this segment or take 1d6 bludgeoning from a falling fragment.
  • The Permission Door's slab visibly tilts. It still works, but the recess is slightly skewed.

Structural 4: Partial Collapse

  • One of the passages partially collapses. The GM chooses which:
    • The Cistern Drain shaft — partly blocked, requires Athletics DC 14 to push through. The bypass becomes much harder.
    • The Collapsed Side Passage (Subsurface Location 12) — further collapse buries Garrold Eft's body and any remaining evidence there.
    • The Old Guardroom entrance — partly blocked; combat in the chamber is harder.
  • The party can hear stones shifting at irregular intervals. Background tension rises.

Structural 5: Major Collapse

  • One of the surface entries closes entirely. The party can no longer return the way they came.
  • The Permission Door's mechanism partly fails — the slab opens, but it shudders. It cannot be re-used for return.
  • Dust hangs in the air throughout the complex. Vision is at disadvantage past 30 feet.

Structural 6: Total Failure

  • The threshold complex is unsafe. Major sections collapse over the next in-fiction hour.
  • If the party is on the surface, they must evacuate. If they are subsurface, they must either descend past the Final Threshold Landing (where the structure is older and more stable) or attempt to escape the cistern.
  • The Permission Door seals shut and cannot be re-opened from above. Any PC left in the antechamber is now trapped between the sealed slab and the surface collapse.
  • Phase 9 will need to find an alternate way back to the surface for the party.

Interaction with Lockdown Clock 6

If both clocks reach 6, the site is being deliberately collapsed by cleaners and falling apart on its own. The party has 15 in-fiction minutes to commit to a course: descend, run, or accept that they will be buried and have to dig out. This is the worst-case scenario; design the encounter so it costs but does not kill.

Enemy Presence Sliders

The number and kind of enemies present at Tarnsmere scales with the Lockdown Clock. The table below summarises starting presence at each Lockdown value. The GM should adjust to taste based on party composition and current HP / resources.

Surface enemy counts

LockdownSurface forces
0–11 Silent Scout, 2 Hired Toughs (Yard).
22 Silent Scouts, 3 Hired Toughs, 1 Route-Cleaner Agent.
33 Silent Scouts, 4 Hired Toughs, 2 Route-Cleaner Agents, 1 Route-Cleaner Lead.
43 Silent Scouts, 5 Hired Toughs, 3 Route-Cleaner Agents, 1 Lead, 1 Deep-Route Courier (standby).
54 Silent Scouts, 6 Hired Toughs, 4 Route-Cleaner Agents, 2 Leads, 1 Deep-Route Courier.
6Active demolition; full force plus fuse-setters. No more reinforcements arriving (the site is being burned, not held).

Subsurface enemy counts

LockdownSubsurface forces
0–1None. The Permission Door has been quiet.
21 Route-Cleaner Agent doing a paperwork sweep in the Broken Registry Chamber. Will retreat if engaged in force.
31 Route-Cleaner Agent + 2 Hired Toughs in the Old Guardroom. They expect intruders.
42 Agents + 3 Hired Toughs split between Guardroom and Registry. 1 Silent Scout at the Stairhouse Entry.
52 Agents + 4 Hired Toughs + 1 Lead, plus a Deep-Route Courier transiting through (may engage or evade).
6Full evacuation in progress; subsurface forces are pulling out and setting fuses. Combat at this stage is opportunistic, not planned.

Named enemies introduced in Phase 8

Route-Cleaner Lead (NEW)

A senior route-cleaner. Captain-grade. The Lead has clean hands, paperwork in a leather case, a polished cudgel, and an expensive-but-discreet blade under a long coat. They speak Aerdy commercial-court formal-register and are scrupulous about not killing people they don't have to. They want the party gone, not dead. Use them as a negotiator first, a combatant second.

Deep-Route Courier (NEW)

A specialised courier who travels the route below. They wear heavier gear than surface cleaners — reinforced boots, a route-mask hung at their belt, a writing case with brass clasps. They are obviously transient; they will not stay to fight if engagement is avoidable. If captured alive, they have information about Phase 9. If killed, their writing case contains route notes and (if Lockdown is 4+) a sealed letter intended for delivery below.

Stone-Witness (Permission Door guardian; see Permission Door journal Method E)

The construct that wakes if the door is forced. 38 HP, AC 16, 3 attacks per round. Slow. Defeatable but expensive. It is a complication, not a wall.

Returning enemies from Phase 7

Route-Cleaner Agents, Silent Scouts, and Hired Toughs are familiar to the GM and party from Phase 7. Their stat blocks are unchanged here.

Faction Reactions to Phase 8 Events

Phase 8 events ripple outward into the four major Greyhawk factions the party has been tracking since Phase 4. Each faction reacts differently to a Tarnsmere descent attempt.

The Aerdy Commercial-Court Network

The faction that actually owns the registry phrase, the toll tokens, and the Permissive Survey legacy paperwork. They are not the route-cleaners; they are the bureaucracy that lent the route-cleaners their forms.

Reactions

  • The party uses Method A or B (clean): The Network notices the descent through their old registry channels — they have one functioning paper line that still receives Tarnsmere notifications, though it is rarely watched. One senior clerk reads the notification within a week. The clerk does not act immediately. She files a query.
  • The party uses Method C (Kethren): The Network notes the descent with a channel-attestation. This is “routine.” They do not investigate.
  • The party uses Method D (Korre Papers) without disabling the listening tube: The Network receives a notification flagged as unsanctioned. A clerk opens a quiet investigation. This investigation reaches the party's friends in Phase 9 or 10 as a polite inquiry: “A descent was registered against the Cindren & Vhal firm mark, which has been extinct since 528 CY. Could you assist with clarification?”
  • The party uses Method E (Force): The Network's lintel-mark eventually shows damage. A senior clerk goes to inspect personally (4 in-fiction weeks out). She finds the broken slab. The Network now formally knows the descent was unsanctioned, the door is broken, and the party was responsible. This becomes a Phase 10 problem.
  • The party uses Method F (Bypass): No registry attestation. The Network does not know the descent happened.
  • Elle's key: The Network's older paper trail picks this up specifically: an old, dormant account has been activated for a Class VII descent. This is a curiosity entry, not an alarm. If the Network's senior clerk ever reads it, she may seek out Elle for clarification — benignly, on church business.

Korre's Network (Route-Cleaners and Couriers)

The faction directly opposed to the party at Tarnsmere. They are functionally Korre's operational arm: cleaners on the surface, couriers transiting the route, agents handling paperwork suppression.

Reactions

  • Lockdown 0–2: Korre does not personally know. Local cleaners are managing.
  • Lockdown 3: A Lead has sent a runner to Greyhawk. Korre will know within 24 hours of the runner's arrival. She may dispatch a higher-tier agent (Phase 9 or later complication).
  • Lockdown 4–5: Korre knows. She is calibrating her response. If Kethren is in custody and the party is openly working with her, Korre considers Kethren burned and stops trying to recover her.
  • Lockdown 6: Korre has personally ordered the site burned. This is a major Phase 10 marker — she is willing to sacrifice the Tarnsmere descent point rather than let the party use it cleanly. The route below will be re-routed within months.

The Watch / Civic Authorities (City of Greyhawk)

The party's official friends. Investigator Iren Volkmar (Phase 4+) and her allies in the Watch have been the party's connection to legitimate civic power.

Reactions

  • Any opening method: Iren is not a Tarnsmere authority. She has no formal claim here. She will not send Watch officers to Tarnsmere without strong justification.
  • Lockdown 5+: The party's faces being circulated in Greyhawk City reaches Iren. She covers for them as long as she can, but her cover is finite. The party may receive an “Iren says: get back to the city; we need to talk” message via Aerdy commercial channels (rapid courier).
  • Structural Failure 5+: Tarnsmere will eventually be inspected by civic authorities — quarterly survey, mining-tax inspection, or similar. The damage becomes a Phase 10 record-keeping problem. Iren can help bury it, but it costs her professional capital.

The Order of Saint Cuthbert / Heironeous (Elle's order)

Elle's order has been a passive faction in earlier phases — supportive of Elle but not active in the investigation. Phase 8 changes this for them.

Reactions

  • If Elle uses her iron key: The order is unaware until Elle reports back. When she does — if she does — the order's archivist quietly opens the 374 CY records and confirms what the order has been holding. The order is now formally aware of the descent and the threshold. Their response in Phase 10 depends on Elle's framing.
  • If Elle does not use her key: The order remains unaware. The key returns to its drawer. The thread continues to be quiet.
  • If Elle dies: The order receives word from the party. If they receive the key, they put it back in the archive without knowing what it opens. Sad coda. The thread closes.

The PCs' Personal Threads

The party's individual threads also have faction-like reactions:

  • Cam's halfling network — if Cam confirmed in the Broken Registry Chamber that Hesp Olfair's cousins were admitted via this door, the network mobilises. Phase 9 receives a strong morale push: “Whatever is down there, our people went there. We're not stopping.”
  • Gianni's geographic-erasure thread — if she found Garrold Eft's body, she carries her own attestation. Phase 9 gives her a heavier emotional load and a sharper investigative edge.
  • Kitty's chthonic-recognition thread — if the seventh cut warmed and she touched it, the recognition has now committed. Phase 9 atmosphere shifts for her.
  • Alicia's pact-mark thread — if she went down the Stair Gallery and her mark went cold, her patron is silent now. Phase 9 opens with her without easy magical recourse from her pact.
  • Selvara's stone-dream thread — the seven-cut cairn was the exact image. Phase 9 may give her her own dream-recognition moment at the next major threshold.
  • Elle's iron-key thread — addressed above.

The general principle

Phase 8 is the last phase where the party's surface-world reputation and faction relationships matter as a primary mode of pressure. Phase 9 takes them somewhere those relationships are mostly inert — they have to bring their alliances with them as letters and tokens, not as live political support.
